scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian write in the 2nd-century Roman Empire, satirizing the social role of the 'parasite' within the broader Graeco-Roman elite culture of his time.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), as he describes the parasite's code as a contemporary, albeit satirical, social phenomenon.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The dialogue discusses the social and legal status of parasites as a class within the author's contemporary Roman Imperial society.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), reflecting contemporary observations on the lack of litigation against social dependents.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian describes the expectations of the wealthy class (and their dependency on parasites for status) within the Roman Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE); the 'rule' of wealth-display via guests is contemporary to his audience.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text discusses the physical fitness and social standing of contemporary intellectuals in Roman-era gymnasia.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), reflecting the cultural competition between philosophers and others in his era.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Crato speaks as a representative of the educated elite (pepaideumenoi) within the 2nd-century Roman Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE); the conflict between traditional education and 'low' entertainment is a contemporary theme.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text refers to the established hierarchy of arts and competitions (Agones) within the Roman Imperial cultural sphere.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), when the status of pantomime was being compared to 'classic' 5th-century genres.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The dialogue reflects the behavioral norms for elderly men of status in the Roman Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), reflecting contemporary views on the dignity expected of greying citizens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian explicitly describes the specific practices and military education of the Spartans.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Though Sparta's heyday was earlier, Lucian uses 'Even now' to describe contemporary Lacedaemonian customs in the 2nd century CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text explicitly names the Roman Salii priesthood as an example of elite ritual dance.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian identifies this as a 'Roman dance' contemporary to his own time (c. 165 CE).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Refers to the general Hellenistic and Roman-era practice regarding secrecy in mystery cults like those at Eleusis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), as these mystery cults were active and their secrecy norms were well-known to his audience.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian describes a specific religious practice and selection process occurring at the sanctuary of Delos.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian describes the Delian ritual as an ongoing or at least standard cultural fact of his own time period.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian describes the specific performance requirements of tragic actors in the theaters of his era.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), contrasting contemporary tragic performance with the newer form of pantomime.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This refers to the technical conventions (masks) of the dramatic arts in the 2nd-century Roman world.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), describing the tools used by actors during his lifetime.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Discusses the evolution and contemporary rules of the pantomime genre in the Roman Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), as he explains the current separation of dancer and singer.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian 'prescribes' the educational standard for the pantomime profession in his own society.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), reflecting the high intellectual expectations of the Second Sophistic.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the professional and literary qualifications required for a dancer to gain legitimacy in the Roman East.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), when pantomime had reached its peak of mythographic complexity.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim cites an example from the reign of Nero, within the history of the Roman Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The verbatim explicitly anchors this rule/anecdote to the time of Nero (reigned 54–68 CE); 60 is a representative year.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian identifies these specific regions (Ionia and Pontus) as having a unique norm for high-born participants in Bacchic dance.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), as he describes contemporary regional social customs in the East.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Refers to the professional 'regulations' of the pantomime art widely practiced across the Roman Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), as he describes the criteria for evaluating contemporary performers.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text focuses specifically on the audience behavior and social 'regulation' in the city of Antioch.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE), as he records the contemporary reputation of Antioch's sharp-tongued theater audiences.
